content.problemStatement:
Chef Ceil has some matchsticks in his kitchen.

**Detail of matchsticks:**

There are NNN matchsticks in total. They are numbered from to 000 to N−1N-1N−1 inclusive. The ithi^{th}ith matchstick takes bib_{i}bi​ time to burn when lighted at one end, and it burns at a uniform rate.

If lighted at both ends simultaneously, the matchstick will take only half of the original time to burn down.

**Arrangement:**

He ties rear end of the all the matchsticks together at one point and the front end is kept free. The matchstick numbered iii is adjacent to matchstick numbered i+1i+1i+1 for all 0≤i≤N−20 \leq i \leq N-20≤i≤N−2.

Bodies of matchsticks do not touch each other, except at the rear end.

**Task:**

There are QQQ queries, in each query we ask:
If he lights the free end of all matchsticks numbered between LLL and RRR inclusive, what will be the time needed for all matchsticks to get completely burnt?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the number of matchsticks. Second line contains N space‑separated integers b_i, the time (in seconds) each matchstick i takes to burn when lit at one end. Third line contains integer Q, the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two integers L and R (0‑based, inclusive) describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single line containing the time (in seconds) after which all matchsticks are completely burnt. The answer must be printed as a floating point number with exactly one digit after the decimal point.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There is only one matchstick, and it takes 5 seconds to burn.

The first matchstick takes 3 seconds to burn, and the second matchstick takes 5 seconds to burn. When lighted at both ends, the second matchstick will burn in 2.5 seconds, which is less than the time it takes for the first matchstick to burn.
The matchsticks in the range [4, 10] will burn in 9 seconds, which is the maximum time among all matchsticks.

solution.approach:
For a query [L,R] every matchstick i in the range is lit at both ends, therefore it burns in b_i/2 seconds. Matchsticks outside the range are lit only at the rear end and need the full b_i seconds. The total time for the query is the maximum of these two groups:
    answer = max( max_{i∉[L,R]} b_i ,  max_{i∈[L,R]} (b_i/2) ).
We need fast retrieval of the maximum b_i in any interval. A segment tree (or a sparse table) built on the original array provides O(log N) range‑maximum queries. Additionally we keep two prefix‑maximum arrays to obtain the maximum outside the interval in O(1):
    leftMax = (L>0) ? prefixMax[L-1] : -∞
    rightMax = (R+1<N) ? suffixMax[R+1] : -∞
    outsideMax = max(leftMax, rightMax).
The inside maximum is obtained from the segment tree. The final answer is max(outsideMax, insideMax/2). All operations are O(log N) per query, O(N) preprocessing per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + Q log N) per test case (building the segment tree takes O(N), each query O(log N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the segment tree, prefix and suffix arrays.
